title: Voltaire
summary: French Enlightenment writer, philosopher, and polemicist who wielded wit and reason as weapons against religious intolerance, judicial torture, and arbitrary power — whose Candide (1759) remains the most devastating satire of optimism ever written, and whose campaign for the victims of religious persecution helped establish the modern concept of civil liberties.
hook: Voltaire was imprisoned twice, exiled three times, and had his books burned in several countries. He responded each time by writing more books. For sixty years he was the most dangerous writer in Europe — not because he commanded armies but because he made powerful people look stupid.

beginner.theFigure:
In 1762, a writer living in a country house near the Swiss border heard about a man who had been tortured and executed for a crime he almost certainly did not commit. Most people would have felt sorry and moved on. Voltaire spent three years fighting to clear the dead man's name. He won.

Voltaire was a French writer who lived from 1694 to 1778. He wrote plays, poems, history books, and a famous short novel called Candide. He also wrote thousands of letters to kings, queens, and scholars across Europe. He used his pen as a weapon against injustice, cruelty, and religious intolerance.

The authorities were not fans. He was imprisoned in the Bastille, a famous Paris prison, twice. He was forced to leave France three separate times. His books were burned in several countries. Each time, he responded by writing more books.

Voltaire became famous across Europe as the wittiest and most dangerous writer of his age. He was dangerous not because he had an army but because he could make powerful people look ridiculous.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Voltaire lived in a France where the Catholic Church had enormous power over everyday life. Books could be burned by the authorities. People could be imprisoned for saying the wrong thing about religion. The legal system used torture to get confessions.

Jews, Protestants, and people who questioned religion lived under constant suspicion. Freethinkers, people who wanted to think for themselves, had to be very careful.

Voltaire believed this was all wrong. He thought people should be free to follow their own religion, or no religion at all, without being punished for it. He called this religious tolerance.

His main tool was not a sword or a petition. It was mockery. He was so good at making his enemies look foolish and cruel that readers across Europe laughed and then started to agree with him.
beginner.centralIdea:
Voltaire's most famous book, Candide, is a short comic novel. It follows a young man named Candide who travels the world and encounters disaster after disaster. Earthquakes hit. Wars happen. Innocent people are enslaved or executed.

Through all of this, Candide's teacher, Pangloss, keeps insisting that everything is fine. 'All is for the best in the best of all possible worlds,' he says, no matter how bad things get.

Voltaire was making a serious point through comedy. He thought it was cruel to tell suffering people that their pain was part of a grand cosmic plan. He wanted people to stop explaining away suffering and start doing something about it.

At the end of the book, Candide stops arguing about philosophy. Instead he says: 'We must cultivate our garden.' That means: stop debating big theories and start fixing actual problems in front of you.

Voltaire also had a phrase he used like a battle cry: 'Écrasez l'infâme,' which means 'Crush the infamous thing.' The infamous thing was religious fanaticism — the use of religion to hurt, oppress, or kill people.
beginner.howSpread:
Voltaire was a genius at getting his ideas out into the world. He used every method available to him.

He wrote for the theatre, where his plays were seen by large audiences. He wrote short, portable books that could be hidden in a coat pocket and passed from hand to hand. He kept up a correspondence, a regular exchange of letters, with thousands of people, including kings and emperors.

He also used a clever trick: he published his most dangerous books under fake names. When the authorities accused him, he denied writing them. This was risky but it worked. His banned books still reached readers all across Europe.

In his final two decades, he lived at a place called Ferney, a country estate on the border between France and Switzerland. From there, French authorities could not easily reach him. He ran what was almost a one-man news operation from his estate. Visitors came from across Europe to meet the great man.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Voltaire's reputation is not simple. People disagree about what he really stood for.

He championed tolerance and fought for the victims of religious persecution. But his own writings contain hateful remarks about Jewish people. He repeated old stereotypes about Jews as greedy and backward. This is a serious problem. A man who argued for tolerance was intolerant himself.

When he died in 1778, France was heading toward revolution. The revolutionaries later moved his remains to the Pantheon, a grand building in Paris where French heroes are honored. They claimed him as one of their founding thinkers.

But Voltaire was a reformer, not a revolutionary. He believed in fixing the system, not overthrowing it. The violence of the French Revolution, where thousands were executed in the name of reason and virtue, was exactly the kind of fanaticism he had spent his life opposing.

His ideas about tolerance and free thought still matter. His prejudices also still matter. Both are part of his legacy.

general.theFigure:
In 1762, a Protestant merchant named Jean Calas was tortured and executed in Toulouse, accused of murdering his own son to stop a conversion to Catholicism. The evidence was thin. The atmosphere was one of anti-Protestant panic. The case attracted little attention outside the region. Voltaire read about it from his estate near Geneva, investigated by correspondence, and decided Calas was innocent. He then spent three years producing pamphlets, lobbying influential figures across Europe, and building enough public pressure to force a royal review. In 1765, the Royal Council reversed the verdict and cleared the Calas family. Voltaire was sixty-eight.

The Calas affair shows what made Voltaire matter. He was not a deep or rigorous philosopher. Hume and Kant surpassed him in that regard. But he was remarkably effective at changing minds, and in the Enlightenment project of practical reform, effectiveness counted for a great deal.
general.worldOfIdeas:
The France Voltaire wrote in gave the Catholic Church broad authority over intellectual life. Books deemed heretical could be burned. Authors could be imprisoned. Courts used torture to extract confessions, and religious minorities lived under legal suspicion. Protestants, Jews, and freethinkers all faced varying degrees of official discrimination.

Voltaire attacked these conditions through ridicule and narrative more than through formal argument. He was contemptuous of system-building in philosophy. His target was not abstract error but specific cruelty, and his method was to make that cruelty look absurd.

His early book, Letters Concerning the English Nation, published in 1733, brought Locke's ideas, Newton's physics, and the relative religious tolerance of English life to French readers. The French authorities burned it. They were not misreading it: the book was an implied argument that English institutions were better than French ones because they allowed more freedom of thought.
general.centralIdea:
Candide, published in 1759, takes aim at a philosophical position associated with the German thinker Leibniz. Leibniz had argued that God, being all-powerful and good, must have created the world in an optimal configuration. Apparent evils were either necessary to prevent greater ones, or contributing to goods too large for us to see. The tutor Pangloss embodies this view in the novel. He insists that all is for good even as the characters face disaster after disaster.

Voltaire's skepticism about this view was sharpened by the Lisbon earthquake of 1755, which killed many thousands of people. He thought Leibniz's argument was not just wrong but harmful: it asked people who suffered to console themselves with the idea that their pain served a cosmic purpose beyond their understanding.

The ending of Candide is often misread. When Candide says 'We must cultivate our garden,' it sounds like a retreat. But the point is more practical: stop trying to solve the whole world-system and instead work on the real problem in front of you. His Treatise on Tolerance, written during the Calas campaign, made the same turn toward the specific case over the grand theory.
general.howSpread:
Voltaire spent roughly his final two decades at Ferney, a country estate on the French-Swiss border, outside the easy reach of French authorities. From there he kept up a large correspondence with Frederick the Great of Prussia, Catherine the Great of Russia, and intellectual figures across Europe. Ferney became a destination; visitors came from across the continent to see him. He was, in effect, running a one-person publishing and advocacy operation from his estate.

His Philosophical Dictionary, published in 1764, was designed to circulate. It was compact and portable, a handbook of freethinking that could be passed from reader to reader without attracting notice. He published much of his controversial work under false names and denied authorship when pressed. This was a practical strategy for getting banned ideas past the censors.

The model he built proved influential beyond France. When Emile Zola published his open letter J'Accuse in 1898 to defend the wrongly convicted officer Alfred Dreyfus, he was drawing on the same approach Voltaire had used in the Calas campaign: use public attention to force institutions to review a specific injustice.
general.contestedInheritance:
Voltaire's antisemitic writing is a serious challenge to his universalist reputation. His Philosophical Dictionary and other works describe Jewish people using longstanding European stereotypes, portraying them as parochial, greedy, and superstitious. His hostility to Judaism was partly driven by his opposition to all revealed religion, but it also drew on racial contempt that went beyond theological criticism. A writer who championed tolerance while expressing this kind of prejudice presents a genuine problem, not one that can be set aside by pointing to his better arguments.

One influential reading of Voltaire places him in what might be called a cautious or moderate strand of Enlightenment thought. On this view, he was committed to religious tolerance and freedom of expression, but he ultimately worked within the social order rather than challenging it at its foundations. A more radical tendency in Enlightenment thought, associated with thinkers like Spinoza, pushed further toward democratic equality and was more skeptical of hierarchy. Voltaire's limits, on this reading, were not accidental but were choices about how far reform should go.

The French revolutionaries moved his remains to the Pantheon in 1791 and claimed him as a founding figure of the republic. The appropriation was selective. Voltaire supported reforming monarchy from within, not replacing it. The mass executions that followed the Revolution, carried out in the name of reason and virtue, were the kind of fanatical certainty he had spent his life opposing.

advanced.theFigure:
The scholarly debate about Voltaire keeps returning to the same tension: his effectiveness as a writer depended on qualities — compression, wit, unfairness, oversimplification — that are antithetical to philosophical rigor. The Voltaire who appears in the history of philosophy is more systematic than the actual Voltaire — the magpie empiricist who borrowed from Locke, Newton, Bayle, and whoever else served his polemical purposes, and who was contemptuous of system precisely because system was the mode of the enemies he was attacking.

Nicholas Cronk's work — including the Oxford Voltaire Foundation's critical edition of his complete works — has been most important in recovering the full range and texture of Voltaire's output, which is far larger and more various than the canonical texts (Candide, the Philosophical Dictionary, the Calas affair pamphlets) suggest.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Voltaire's relationship to the English tradition deserves more attention than it typically receives. His eighteen months in England (1726-28), following his imprisonment in the Bastille and exile, were formative: he encountered Locke's empiricism, Newton's physics, the relative tolerance of English religious pluralism, and the commercial prosperity that he associated with the freedom of thought and trade. The Letters Concerning the English Nation (1733) — known in French as Lettres philosophiques — introduced this world to French readers and was immediately burned by the French authorities as an attack on existing institutions.

The Letters represent Voltaire's most systematic statement of his intellectual commitments: empiricism over metaphysics, religious pluralism over Catholic monopoly, commercial society over aristocratic hierarchy. The argument was philosophical but also political: English institutions were better because they allowed for intellectual and religious freedom, and that freedom produced prosperity as well as truth.
advanced.centralIdea:
The depth of Voltaire's quarrel with Leibnizian optimism is easy to miss in the comedy of Candide. The Leibnizian position was not merely philosophical cheerfulness — it was a metaphysical claim with real moral consequences: if the world is rationally optimized by an all-knowing God, then the suffering of individuals is either instrumentally necessary or a failure of our perception. Voltaire understood the 1755 Lisbon earthquake as a direct refutation. Tens of thousands died. No theodicy made that tolerable, and he thought the attempt to make it tolerable was itself a kind of cruelty.

The ending of Candide has divided readers ever since. 'We must cultivate our garden' is most often read as withdrawal, a retreat from the philosophical ambition of the novel's first three quarters. The stronger reading is that Voltaire was proposing a different standard for moral seriousness: not the construction of a total theory that justifies or condemns the cosmos, but patient attention to the improvable situation immediately before you. The Calas pamphlets make the same move in a political register. What unites them is a suspicion of grand justification — whether metaphysical or political — and a preference for the particular wrong that can actually be addressed.
advanced.howSpread:
The international circulation of Voltaire's works was enabled by the clandestine publishing networks of the Enlightenment — the Swiss and Dutch publishers who produced works that could not be printed in France and smuggled them back across the border. Voltaire was not unique in using these networks, but he was uniquely skilled at managing them: maintaining deniability while ensuring that his banned works reached their audiences.

The Ferney model of the public intellectual — operating from outside the jurisdiction of the state being criticized, using international attention as leverage — has had an extremely long run. Zola's J'Accuse (1898) during the Dreyfus affair was explicitly modeled on Voltaire's Calas intervention. Amnesty International's methodology of international public pressure on specific cases of injustice is a secularized version of the same approach.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The most productive recent debate about Voltaire concerns his relationship to the Enlightenment's legacy of universalism and its failures. Jonathan Israel's argument — that Voltaire represents a 'moderate Enlightenment' that ultimately accepted social hierarchies it should have challenged — has been influential but contested. The specific question of his antisemitism has been addressed by scholars including Adam Sutcliffe, who argues that Voltaire's hostility to Judaism was integral to his anti-religious project rather than incidental to it: he needed to attack the Old Testament, and his attacks slid from the text to its bearers.

Whether the Enlightenment's failures — its tolerance of slavery, its racial hierarchies, its gendered exclusions — are best understood as betrayals of its principles or as constitutive features of those principles is the deepest question in Enlightenment studies, and Voltaire is a central exhibit in the debate.

foundation.theStory: Voltaire was the Enlightenment's greatest polemicist — the figure who translated philosophical commitments into forms that could change minds and institutions. His campaign for the victims of religious persecution, culminating in the Calas affair, invented the model of the public intellectual as defender of civil liberties. Candide remains the most penetrating satire of the philosophical optimism that justified present suffering in the name of cosmic purpose.
foundation.theDebate: Scholars debate whether Voltaire's 'moderate Enlightenment' was a principled philosophical position or a strategic compromise with existing hierarchies; how his antisemitism and racial prejudices relate to his universalist rhetoric; whether Candide's 'cultivate the garden' is quietism or a more sophisticated philosophical position; and his relationship to the radical Enlightenment of Spinoza and d'Holbach that Israel has distinguished from Voltaire's more cautious liberalism.
foundation.whyItMatters: Voltaire matters as the inventor of the public intellectual as a social role — the writer who uses public attention to intervene in specific cases of injustice. He matters as the primary popularizer of Enlightenment ideas across European educated society. And he matters as a test case for the Enlightenment's internal contradictions: a champion of tolerance who expressed intolerance, a universalist whose universalism had limits.
questions: Voltaire's 'cultivate your garden' is often read as a retreat from engagement into private life. But it can also be read as an argument that practical action on specific human problems is more valuable than metaphysical system-building. Which reading is more accurate, and what is at stake in the choice?

Voltaire made antisemitic statements while championing religious tolerance. Does this contradiction undermine his Enlightenment project, or is it best understood as a specific failing within a generally important intellectual tradition?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Voltaire scholarship divides between literary historians focused on his specific works and intellectual historians focused on his place in the Enlightenment. Nicholas Cronk's editorial work at the Oxford Voltaire Foundation is the foundation for any serious engagement with the full corpus. Roger Pearson's biography is the most accessible scholarly account. Jonathan Israel's three-volume Enlightenment history situates Voltaire within a broader argument about the moderate versus radical Enlightenment. The antisemitism debate has been addressed by Adam Sutcliffe and others working on Jewish-Enlightenment relations.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The primary evidence is enormous — Voltaire's complete works run to approximately thirty thousand pages, and his correspondence alone fills fifteen thousand letters. The Oxford critical edition is the authoritative text. The limits: Voltaire's strategic use of pseudonymity and deniability complicates attribution in some cases; the relationship between his public positions and private beliefs requires careful reading of the correspondence.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Voltaire must engage with both his genuine contributions and his specific failures — not using the failures to dismiss the contributions, or the contributions to excuse the failures. The antisemitism question is not peripheral: understanding how a champion of tolerance could express intolerance toward Jews illuminates the specific limits of Enlightenment universalism and has implications for how we understand the tradition's legacy.
questions: Voltaire used mockery, satire, and narrative rather than systematic philosophical argument as his primary tools. Is this a philosophical weakness or a different kind of philosophical strength — a recognition that reason's most effective mode is sometimes wit rather than rigor?

The public intellectual model Voltaire pioneered — using international public attention to intervene in specific cases of injustice — has been enormously influential. What are its limits as well as its strengths?

research.topic: The Calas affair and its significance for civil liberties
research.content: Historical scholarship has established both the details of the Calas case and the significance of Voltaire's intervention. Jean Calas's posthumous rehabilitation in 1765 by the Royal Council was a direct result of Voltaire's campaign. The case produced several pamphlets that are among Voltaire's most significant political writings — particularly the Traité sur la tolérance (Treatise on Tolerance, 1763). The case is now recognized as a foundational episode in the history of civil liberties advocacy and public intellectual intervention.

Scholarly analysis has established that Voltaire's writings contain significant antisemitic passages — in the Philosophical Dictionary, in his historical works, and in correspondence. The passages draw on both traditional Christian anti-Judaism and secular stereotypes. Adam Sutcliffe's Judaism and Enlightenment has established that Voltaire's hostility to Judaism was integral to his anti-religious project rather than incidental: he needed to discredit the Old Testament as a source of religious authority, and his attacks slid from the text to its bearers.

research.topic: The full extent of Voltaire's anonymous and pseudonymous publication
research.content: Voltaire published extensively under false names or anonymously throughout his career. The Oxford critical edition has resolved the attribution of most major works, but the full extent of his clandestine publication cannot be definitively established. What evidence exists: the critical apparatus of the Oxford edition, which uses internal evidence, contemporaneous attributions, and archival research to establish authorship. What cannot be fully recovered: the smaller pamphlets, the ephemeral pieces, and the works that may have been destroyed or that never entered the archival record.

comparativeNarrative.name: French Revolutionary Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The French revolutionaries moved Voltaire's remains to the Panthéon in 1791 and claimed him as one of their founding thinkers. But their Voltaire was not quite the real one. He was a reformer, not a revolutionary. He believed in fixing the existing system, not tearing it down. The Terror — the mass execution of thousands in the name of reason and virtue — was exactly the kind of fanaticism he had spent his career opposing. The revolutionaries used his name while ignoring the part of his thinking that would have led him to oppose them.

comparativeNarrative.name: Catholic and Religious Conservative Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For Catholic and religious conservative thinkers, Voltaire is the man who helped pull apart the foundations of European Christian civilization. His battle cry — 'Crush the infamous thing,' aimed at the Church — was not just a clever slogan. It shaped how educated Europeans came to see religion as something to be pushed out of public life. Whether that shift was progress or loss is a real question. Critics in this tradition argue that Voltaire attacked the moral and intellectual roots of the culture he claimed to be improving, and that the Revolution's violence showed what happened when reason cut itself free from religious tradition.

comparativeNarrative.name: Contemporary Enlightenment Defenders and Critics
comparativeNarrative.content: Voltaire has become a battleground in current arguments about the Enlightenment's legacy. Those who defend the Enlightenment point to his work for tolerance, free expression, and civil liberties. Those who challenge it point to his antisemitism, his acceptance of racial hierarchies, and his link to a tradition that used the word 'civilization' to justify colonial conquest. Both sides are picking what they need. The actual Voltaire was more complicated than either picture — neither the clean liberal hero nor the simple villain that each side requires.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Voltaire was the Enlightenment's most effective popularizer — the figure who translated philosophical commitments into forms that reached and changed the educated public of Europe.

rabbitHole.label: Rousseau
rabbitHole.reason: The Voltaire-Rousseau conflict was the Enlightenment's defining internal debate — between reason and nature, civilization and corruption, the philosophe and the prophet.

rabbitHole.label: The French Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Voltaire's writings contributed to the intellectual climate that produced the Revolution — though the Revolution's violence would have appalled him.

rabbitHole.label: Tolerance
rabbitHole.reason: Voltaire's Treatise on Tolerance is one of the foundational texts in the philosophy of religious tolerance — his practical campaign for the Calas family gave the abstract argument specific human weight.

rabbitHole.label: Secularism
rabbitHole.reason: Voltaire's sustained attack on the political power of organized religion was a primary driver of European secularization — the separation of civil authority from religious authority that defines modern liberal states.

reference.author: Voltaire
reference.title: Candide, or Optimism
reference.annotation: The essential starting point — read in Theo Cuffe's Penguin translation, which best captures the pace and wit of the original. Two hours well spent before engaging any secondary account.

reference.author: Voltaire
reference.title: Treatise on Tolerance
reference.annotation: Voltaire's serious voice on the subject he cared most about — written during the Calas affair campaign, it remains one of the most powerful arguments for religious tolerance in the philosophical tradition.

reference.author: Pearson, Roger
reference.title: Voltaire Almighty: A Life in Pursuit of Freedom
reference.annotation: The best scholarly biography in English — Pearson captures both the range of Voltaire's output and the relentlessness of his pursuit of reform. Accessible to general readers while satisfying to specialists.

reference.author: Israel, Jonathan
reference.title: Enlightenment Contested: Philosophy, Modernity, and the Emancipation of Man 1670-1752
reference.annotation: The essential comparative context — Israel's argument that Voltaire represents a 'moderate Enlightenment' in contrast to the more radical tradition of Spinoza is the most important recent reframing of his significance.

reference.author: Sutcliffe, Adam
reference.title: Judaism and Enlightenment
reference.annotation: The essential account of Voltaire's antisemitism and its relationship to his anti-religious project — Sutcliffe establishes that the hostility to Judaism was integral, not accidental, to the Enlightenment critique of revealed religion.

reference.author: Cronk, Nicholas (ed.)
reference.title: The Cambridge Companion to Voltaire
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly reference — Cronk's collection covers the full range of Voltaire's output with contributions from leading specialists. The best starting point for serious scholarly engagement with specific aspects of his work.

reference.author: Voltaire
reference.title: Letters Concerning the English Nation (Lettres philosophiques)
reference.annotation: The primary text the entry treats as Voltaire's most systematic statement of his commitments — empiricism, religious pluralism, commercial society — and whose burning by French authorities the account narrates directly.

reference.author: Voltaire
reference.title: Philosophical Dictionary (Dictionnaire philosophique)
reference.annotation: The portable freethinking handbook the entry narrates as designed for clandestine circulation, and the work it names as a primary site of Voltaire's antisemitic passages.

reference.author: Cronk, Nicholas (ed.)
reference.title: Œuvres complètes de Voltaire (Oxford Voltaire Foundation critical edition)
reference.annotation: The authoritative critical edition the entry repeatedly invokes as the basis for recovering the full corpus and resolving the attribution of Voltaire's pseudonymous and anonymous works.

commerce.title: Candide
commerce.author: Voltaire
commerce.note: Read this first — it takes two hours, it's funny and devastating, and it's the best introduction to what Voltaire was and why he mattered. The Theo Cuffe Penguin translation is the best available.

commerce.title: Voltaire Almighty
commerce.author: Roger Pearson
commerce.note: The best biography for general readers — Pearson captures the range and energy of Voltaire's career without losing sight of the specific battles he was fighting.

commerce.title: Treatise on Tolerance
commerce.author: Voltaire
commerce.note: Voltaire's serious voice — written during the Calas campaign, it is the most direct statement of his commitment to religious tolerance and the most accessible of his philosophical works.

commerce.title: Letters Concerning the English Nation
commerce.author: Voltaire
commerce.note: The work that introduced Locke, Newton, and English religious pluralism to French readers — and was immediately burned. Essential for understanding Voltaire's intellectual sources and his method.

commerce.title: The Cambridge Companion to Voltaire
commerce.author: Nicholas Cronk (ed.)
commerce.note: The essential scholarly reference — covers the full range of Voltaire's output with contributions from leading specialists. The starting point for serious engagement with specific aspects of his work.

commerce.title: Judaism and Enlightenment
commerce.author: Adam Sutcliffe
commerce.note: Essential for understanding the relationship between Voltaire's universalism and his specific prejudices — Sutcliffe's account of the antisemitism in the Enlightenment tradition is indispensable.

popularCulture.title: Candide (1759) — as a living reference
popularCulture.description: 'Cultivate your garden' and 'all is for the best in the best of all possible worlds' are now everyday jokes about blind optimism. People quote them to mock anyone who stays cheerful in a mess. Voltaire meant something harder than a joke. He argued that telling suffering people their pain serves a grand plan is its own kind of cruelty.

popularCulture.title: Candide — Leonard Bernstein (1956)
popularCulture.description: Bernstein turned the short novel into a Broadway musical, and the score moves with the same wit and speed as the book. It plays disaster for comedy and song. The show shrinks the story into a tale of lost innocence. That softens Voltaire's harder claim: cheerful optimism, told to people in pain, becomes a weapon against them.

popularCulture.title: Voltaire and free speech
popularCulture.description: The line people love — 'I disapprove of what you say, but I will defend to the death your right to say it' — came from his biographer Evelyn Beatrice Hall in 1906. Voltaire never wrote it. People keep handing it to him anyway. The name 'Voltaire' now stands for free speech itself, floating free of the actual man.

popularCulture.title: Écrasez l'infâme in contemporary secularism
popularCulture.description: Voltaire's slogan 'Crush the infamous thing' aimed at the grip of the Catholic Church on French life. Today people borrow it for fights over secularism and blasphemy laws. The Church drops out of the picture. What is left is a broad attack on religion in public life, wider than Voltaire meant. He was a deist who believed in God.

popularCulture.title: The Panthéon and revolutionary appropriation
popularCulture.description: In 1791 the revolutionaries carried Voltaire's bones to the Panthéon and named him a founder of the republic. The honor was real, but the politics were not his. He wanted to repair the monarchy, not end it, and he was no democrat. The ceremony made him a banner for ideas he would have pushed back against.

comparativeSummary: Everyone who remembers Voltaire seems to want a Voltaire built to their own purpose, and the disagreement is really a fight over who gets to claim him. Revolutionaries put his bones in the Panthéon and called him a founder, though he wanted to mend the monarchy, not topple it. Religious conservatives blame him for hollowing out Christian Europe. Today's defenders of the Enlightenment cite his fight for tolerance, while its critics point to his contempt for Jews. Each side keeps the half it needs and drops the rest. The man underneath fits none of these portraits cleanly.
